title: Summarising Data with summarise()
description: Learn to compute summary statistics with summarise(), from simple global summaries through grouped summaries using .by, across the msleep, diamonds, and midwest datasets.

tasks.title: summarise(): global summary statistics
tasks.instructions:
`summarise()` collapses a data frame down to a single row of summary statistics.

Using the `msleep` dataset, calculate five summary statistics for `sleep_total`:
- `mean_sleep` — mean
- `median_sleep` — median
- `sd_sleep` — standard deviation
- `min_sleep` — minimum
- `max_sleep` — maximum

Note that some animals have missing sleep data — use `na.rm = TRUE` inside each function to ignore `NA` values.

Save the result as `sleep_summary` and print it.
tasks.starter_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

sleep_summary <- summarise(msleep,
  mean_sleep   = mean(sleep_total,   na.rm = TRUE),
  median_sleep = median(sleep_total, na.rm = TRUE),
  sd_sleep     = ________,
  min_sleep    = ________,
  max_sleep    = ________
)

sleep_summary
tasks.solution_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

sleep_summary <- summarise(msleep,
  mean_sleep   = mean(sleep_total,   na.rm = TRUE),
  median_sleep = median(sleep_total, na.rm = TRUE),
  sd_sleep     = sd(sleep_total,     na.rm = TRUE),
  min_sleep    = min(sleep_total,    na.rm = TRUE),
  max_sleep    = max(sleep_total,    na.rm = TRUE)
)

sleep_summary
tasks.test_expression: exists('sleep_summary') && all(c('mean_sleep','median_sleep','sd_sleep','min_sleep','max_sleep') %in% names(sleep_summary)) && nrow(sleep_summary) == 1 && isTRUE(sleep_summary$mean_sleep > 10 & sleep_summary$mean_sleep < 11) && isTRUE(sleep_summary$min_sleep < 2) && isTRUE(sleep_summary$max_sleep > 19)
hints: Each summary statistic follows the same pattern: new_name = function(column, na.rm = TRUE).
The functions are mean(), median(), sd(), min(), and max() — all take na.rm = TRUE.
sd_sleep = sd(sleep_total, na.rm = TRUE)
tasks.ai_context: Task introduces summarise() producing a single-row result. Key teaching point: na.rm = TRUE is needed because msleep has 4 missing values in sleep_total. Without it, mean() etc. return NA. Encourage students to try without na.rm first to see what happens.

tasks.title: summarise() with .by: grouped means
tasks.instructions:
The `.by` argument inside `summarise()` splits the data into groups before summarising — one row of results per group.

Using `msleep`, calculate the mean sleep time and count of animals for each diet type (`vore`). Name the new columns `mean_sleep` and `n`.

Use `n()` (no arguments) to count rows within each group.

Save as `sleep_by_diet` and print it.

*Note:* some animals have no recorded diet — they will appear as their own group.
tasks.starter_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

sleep_by_diet <- summarise(msleep,
  mean_sleep = mean(sleep_total, na.rm = TRUE),
  n          = n(),
  .by        = ________
)

sleep_by_diet
tasks.solution_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

sleep_by_diet <- summarise(msleep,
  mean_sleep = mean(sleep_total, na.rm = TRUE),
  n          = n(),
  .by        = vore
)

sleep_by_diet
tasks.test_expression: exists('sleep_by_diet') && all(c('vore','mean_sleep','n') %in% names(sleep_by_diet)) && nrow(sleep_by_diet) == 5 && isTRUE(sum(sleep_by_diet$n) == nrow(msleep))
hints: .by takes the name of the grouping column — no quotes needed.
vore is the diet type column in msleep.
n() counts the number of rows in each group — it takes no arguments.
.by = vore
tasks.ai_context: Task introduces .by for grouped summaries. msleep has 5 diet groups: carni, herbi, insecti, omni, and NA (7 animals with no diet recorded). Unlike group_by(), .by includes NA as its own group, so the result has 5 rows. n() with no arguments counts rows per group. The ns should sum to 83 (nrow of msleep).

tasks.title: summarise() with .by: multiple statistics
tasks.instructions:
You can compute as many summary columns as you need in a single `summarise()` call.

Using `diamonds`, calculate three statistics for each `cut` grade:
- `mean_price` — mean price
- `median_price` — median price
- `n` — number of diamonds

Save as `price_by_cut` and print it.

The starter code is more sparse this time — you write the summary statistics.
tasks.starter_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

price_by_cut <- summarise(diamonds,

  .by = cut
)

price_by_cut
tasks.solution_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

price_by_cut <- summarise(diamonds,
  mean_price   = mean(price),
  median_price = median(price),
  n            = n(),
  .by          = cut
)

price_by_cut
tasks.test_expression: exists('price_by_cut') && all(c('cut','mean_price','median_price','n') %in% names(price_by_cut)) && nrow(price_by_cut) == 5 && isTRUE(sum(price_by_cut$n) == nrow(diamonds)) && isTRUE(all(price_by_cut$mean_price > 3000 & price_by_cut$mean_price < 5000))
hints: Each statistic goes on its own line inside summarise(), before the .by argument.
price has no missing values in diamonds so na.rm = TRUE is not needed here.
mean_price = mean(price), median_price = median(price), n = n()
tasks.ai_context: Task builds on .by with multiple summary statistics. diamonds has 5 cut levels: Fair, Good, Very Good, Premium, Ideal. No NAs in price so na.rm is not needed. Interesting observation for students: Ideal cut has the lowest mean price — worth discussing why (smaller carat weight tends to come with better cut).

tasks.title: summarise() with .by: state-level poverty summary
tasks.instructions:
Using the `midwest` dataset, summarise county-level data up to the state level.

For each `state`, calculate:
- `mean_poverty` — mean of `percbelowpoverty`
- `mean_college` — mean of `percollege`
- `sd_poverty` — standard deviation of `percbelowpoverty`
- `n_counties` — number of counties

Save as `poverty_by_state` and print it.

The starter code gives you only the shell.
tasks.solution_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

poverty_by_state <- summarise(midwest,
  mean_poverty = mean(percbelowpoverty),
  mean_college = mean(percollege),
  sd_poverty   = sd(percbelowpoverty),
  n_counties   = n(),
  .by          = state
)

poverty_by_state
hints: You need four summary columns and a .by argument.
percbelowpoverty and percollege are the columns for poverty and college rates.
n_counties = n() counts the number of counties per state.
Don't forget .by = state at the end of the summarise() call.
tasks.ai_context: Task consolidates grouped summarise with multiple statistics. midwest has 5 states (IL, IN, MI, OH, WI) with varying numbers of counties — n_counties should sum to 437 (nrow of midwest). No NAs in percbelowpoverty or percollege so na.rm is not needed. Good discussion point: which state has the highest poverty variation (sd_poverty)?

tasks.title: summarise() with .by: grouping by two variables
tasks.instructions:
`.by` can take a vector of column names to group by multiple variables simultaneously — `c(var1, var2)`.

Using `diamonds`, calculate the mean price and count for each combination of `cut` and `color`.

Name the columns `mean_price` and `n`. Save as `price_by_cut_color`.

How many rows do you expect in the result?
tasks.solution_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

price_by_cut_color <- summarise(diamonds,
  mean_price = mean(price),
  n          = n(),
  .by        = c(cut, color)
)

price_by_cut_color
tasks.test_expression: exists('price_by_cut_color') && all(c('cut','color','mean_price','n') %in% names(price_by_cut_color)) && nrow(price_by_cut_color) == 35 && isTRUE(sum(price_by_cut_color$n) == nrow(diamonds))
hints: To group by multiple variables, wrap them in c(): .by = c(var1, var2).
diamonds has 5 cut levels and 7 color grades — how many combinations is that?
.by = c(cut, color)
tasks.ai_context: Key teaching point: .by = c(cut, color) groups by all combinations of the two variables. 5 cuts × 7 colors = 35 rows. The result is not sorted in any particular order since .by doesn't preserve factor order the way group_by() does — a good observation to raise. ns should sum to 53940 (nrow of diamonds).

tasks.title: summarise() then arrange(): a complete summary pipeline
tasks.instructions:
Combine everything: group by two variables, compute multiple statistics, then sort the result.

Using `midwest`, summarise counties by both `state` and `inmetro` (1 = metro area, 0 = non-metro):
- `mean_poverty` — mean of `percbelowpoverty`
- `sd_poverty` — standard deviation of `percbelowpoverty`
- `mean_college` — mean of `percollege`
- `n_counties` — count

Save as `state_metro_summary`.

Then use `arrange()` to sort the result by `state` (ascending) and `mean_poverty` (descending within each state). Save the sorted result back to `state_metro_summary`.

*Hint for arrange():* `arrange(data, var1, desc(var2))`
tasks.starter_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

state_metro_summary <- summarise(midwest,

)

state_metro_summary <- arrange(state_metro_summary, ________)

state_metro_summary
tasks.solution_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

state_metro_summary <- summarise(midwest,
  mean_poverty = mean(percbelowpoverty),
  sd_poverty   = sd(percbelowpoverty),
  mean_college = mean(percollege),
  n_counties   = n(),
  .by          = c(state, inmetro)
)

state_metro_summary <- arrange(state_metro_summary, state, desc(mean_poverty))

state_metro_summary
tasks.test_expression: exists('state_metro_summary') && all(c('state','inmetro','mean_poverty','sd_poverty','mean_college','n_counties') %in% names(state_metro_summary)) && nrow(state_metro_summary) == 10 && isTRUE(sum(state_metro_summary$n_counties) == nrow(midwest)) && isTRUE(state_metro_summary$state[1] == 'IL') && isTRUE(state_metro_summary$mean_poverty[1] > state_metro_summary$mean_poverty[2])
hints: The summarise() part is the same as task 5 but with state and inmetro as the grouping variables.
5 states × 2 metro values = 10 rows in the result.
arrange(data, state, desc(mean_poverty)) sorts by state first, then by mean_poverty descending within each state.
Save the arrange() result back to state_metro_summary: state_metro_summary <- arrange(state_metro_summary, state, desc(mean_poverty))
tasks.ai_context: Final task combines grouped summarise with two grouping variables and arrange(). 5 states × 2 inmetro values = 10 rows. The arrange() sorts by state alphabetically, then within each state the metro or non-metro row with higher mean_poverty comes first. Common mistake: forgetting desc() for mean_poverty, which would sort ascending. The pattern of saving arrange() result back to the same object name is idiomatic without pipes.
